title: Mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiFM/PiF-IR) -- Answers to some questions
abstract: Mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiFM/PiF-IR) enables high-resolution chemical imaging of surfaces with lateral resolution less than 5 nm. Here are some answers to questions about the physical background, practical handling and potential applications of PiF-IR including its use in the context of studying antimicrobial interaction. Such questions had been addressed to me during the Faraday Discussions on Vibrations at Interfaces which took place in April 2026 in Manchester/UK. The discussion was part of the theme "What is the question, what is the technique?" in the context of which I presented our recent work [James et al., Faraday Discussions, 2026, doi: 10.1039/d6fd00003g]. A modified version of this manuscript will be published in the themed collection "Vibrations at Interfaces" in Faraday Discussions.
summary: This paper clarifies the physical principles and practical applications of mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiF-IR). It provides strategies for accurate chemical imaging by decoupling field-coupling effects and demonstrates the technique's ability to resolve nanometer-scale chemical changes in biological structures like bacterial cell walls.
main_problem: Addressing technical and physical questions regarding Mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiF-IR), specifically its physical background, practical handling, and potential applications in biological and chemical imaging.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that IR-PiF-IR can resolve chemical composition (e.g., peptidoglycan vs. membrane) by comparing intensities at different frequencies to decouple field-coupling effects, and shows its utility in visualizing antibiotic-induced changes in bacteria.
method: An AFM-based method using tip-enhanced mid-infrared illumination (via QCLs) and heterodyne side-band detection to measure mechanical forces induced by thermal expansion.
model_or_system: The experimental platform involves an AFM tip interacting with various systems including Bacillus subtilis bacteria, PMMA nanoparticles, and molecular monolayers on gold or CaF2 substrates.
key_observables: PiF contrast, spectral line shapes (dissipative vs. dispersive), chemical maps, and spectral shifts in amide bands.
important_parameters: Lateral resolution < 5 nm, spectral range 800-1800 cm-1, and penetration depth ranging from volume to surface-sensitive.
main_assumptions: Assumes the tip-sample interaction force is the sum of conservative and non-conservative forces and that the heterodyne PiF signal is proportional to the gradient of the tip-sample force.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Comparison of PiF-IR scans of B. subtilis using RGB merge for chemical mapping. Fig 2: Classification of AFM-IR methods by resolution/depth and PMMA refractive index plots. Fig 4: Chemometric analysis of bacterial surfaces and spectral signatures of hydrogen bond formation.
paper_structure: The paper is structured as a response to specific technical questions, covering physical mechanisms, comparison of AFM-IR modes, practical application to biological samples (bacteria/exosomes), and discussions on sensitivity and substrate effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 Single illumination frequency PiF contrasts of treated Bacillus subtilis harvested after 15 min. a-c) Topography, d-f) AFM Phase and g-i) PiF. Left: ν = 1520 cm−1, middle: ν = 1060 cm−1, right: RGB with "G" set to ν = 1520 cm−1 and "R+B" set to ν = 1060 cm−1. Schematics in a indicate the light propagation projected onto the sample plane, and in c the illumination geometry including the electric field oscillation in the plane of incidence normal to the sample plane. The yellow arrows in a,b mark a line that is lower in height than the neighboring cell material. Figure reproduced under terms of CC-BY 4.0 license from Ali et al. 11 Copyright ©2025 The Authors.
